content.text: Destructuring is a JavaScript feature that unpacks values from an object into named variables in a single statement. When applied to the props object in React, it eliminates repetitive
prefixes and makes a component's API immediately visible at the top of its function body.

attrs.alt: Three side-by-side panels showing: the props object approach (verbose), destructuring in the function signature (recommended), and destructuring in the body (for when props is also needed)

content.text: Destructuring an object extracts named properties into local variables:
The same syntax works in function parameters — you destructure the argument as it arrives, rather than storing it first:
The preferred React convention is to destructure props directly in the function's parameter list:
The destructured version is shorter and all accepted props are visible at the very top — a reader sees the component's interface without scanning the JSX.
Sometimes you need both the full
object (to spread onto a child) and individual prop names (for local use). In that case, destructure inside the body:
This is equivalent to using rest in the signature, but body-level destructuring is clearer when the logic is more complex.
JavaScript destructuring supports default values with
after each property name. The default applies only when the value is
means "omitted". Destructuring defaults respond only to
Destructuring lets you rename a prop to a different local variable using

The rename is local — the caller still passes the original prop name. This is useful when the prop name collides with a JS keyword or a local variable, or when a more descriptive internal name improves readability.
You can destructure nested object props inline, but keep it shallow — deep nesting reduces readability:
If you need deeply nested data in many places inside the component, destructure it in the body near where it is used rather than cramming everything into the signature.
When a prop is an array and you need specific elements by index, use array destructuring inside the body:
) collects all destructured-but-unnamed props into a new object:
item in the destructure list — placing it elsewhere is a syntax error. The collected object only contains props that were
When a component accepts many props, spread the destructure across lines so each prop is scannable:
The trailing comma after the last prop is valid JavaScript and is recommended — it makes adding a new prop later a one-line diff rather than a two-line diff.
In TypeScript the type annotation goes before the destructure pattern, or inline with an interface:
TypeScript infers default values as the fallback for optional props, so you do not need to repeat the default in the interface. The
marks the prop as optional; the
in the destructure provides its runtime default.
Destructuring in the signature is almost always the right choice. Stick with the props object when:
that forwards all props to a wrapped component:

Useful for HOCs; always have the full object
Verbose; API not visible at top
Long signatures can be hard to scan if many props
Can still use props for spreading; fine-grained control
More boilerplate; API split between two locations
Once props are destructured into local variables, you can derive new values from them before the return statement. This keeps JSX free of complex logic:
with defaults makes the derivation logic clean. The JSX only reads final, ready-to-display values.
Prop Aliases for Cleaner Internal Names
A common pattern is accepting a generic prop name from the outside and using a domain-specific alias inside the component. This decouples the public API from internal wording:
Destructuring with defaults is especially powerful when combined with conditional rendering — optional props get sensible defaults and the JSX stays clean:
, the optional sections render only when the caller provides them. No special checks needed.
Refs cannot be passed as a regular prop — React intercepts them. To expose a ref on a custom component you must use
as separate arguments. Destructuring still applies to
from props while collecting the rest into
for spreading is exactly the same pattern used in regular components.
. Destructuring is applied in the method body rather than the constructor:
You cannot destructure in the class method signature itself; the body is the only option. This is one ergonomic advantage of function components over class components for prop-heavy components.

— naming every field of a large object in the signature when only two are used creates unnecessary noise. Destructure only what the component reads.

Destructure props in the function signature to make the component's API visible and remove repetitive prefixes.

Keep nested destructuring shallow — flat props are easier to read and test.
Format multi-prop destructures with one prop per line and a trailing comma.
